section.title: Applications of triple integrals
section.p: – taught us how to add over a solid. The geometry changed from boxes to wedges, cylinders, cones, balls, and shells, but the basic contribution always had the same shape: \text{value near a point}\times \text{tiny volume}. Now the value near the point becomes physical. It may be mass density, charge density, energy density, or probability density.
Because spherical coordinates already use the letter \rho, this section writes mass density as \delta(x,y,z).

subsection.title: Mass and density in space
subsection.p: Imagine a clear gel block in a laboratory. A chemical additive has settled unevenly, so the density is larger near the top. The block occupies E=[0,4]\times[0,3]\times[0,2], and its density is \delta(x,y,z)=2+z grams per cubic centimeter.

statement.p: If a solid E has mass density \delta(x,y,z), then its mass is \boxed{ M=\iiint_E \delta(x,y,z)\,dV. }

subsection.p: In oriented form notation, the same integral can be written \iiint_E \delta(x,y,z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz, when the solid is given the standard orientation. For mass, however, we usually write dV, because mass uses positive volume.

example.title: Mass of a cone with density depending on height

statement.p:
A solid cone has height 4, radius 4, and density \delta(x,y,z)=1+z. The cone has equation 0\le z\le 4-r in cylindrical coordinates. Thus 0\le \theta\le 2\pi, \qquad 0\le r\le4, \qquad 0\le z\le4-r. The mass is
M \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^4\int_0^{4-r} (1+z)r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta \amp= 2\pi\int_0^4 \left[z+\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_0^{4-r} r\,dr \amp= 2\pi\int_0^4 \left((4-r)+\frac12(4-r)^2\right)r\,dr.
Expand: (4-r)+\frac12(4-r)^2 = 4-r+8-4r+\frac12r^2 = 12-5r+\frac12r^2. So
M \amp= 2\pi\int_0^4 \left(12r-5r^2+\frac12r^3\right)\,dr \amp= 2\pi \left[ 6r^2-\frac53r^3+\frac18r^4 \right]_0^4 \amp= 2\pi \left( 96-\frac{320}{3}+32 \right) \amp= 2\pi\cdot\frac{64}{3} \amp= \frac{128\pi}{3}.
Thus \boxed{ M=\frac{128\pi}{3}. }

subsection.p: The density decides how much each small volume piece counts. The geometry decides where the pieces are.

subsection.title: Center of mass
subsection.p: Mass tells us how much matter there is. The center of mass tells us where that matter balances.

Return to the gel block E=[0,4]\times[0,3]\times[0,2], with density \delta(x,y,z)=2+z. The density depends only on height. So symmetry already tells us \bar x=2, \qquad \bar y=\frac32. But the z-coordinate of the center should be above the geometric middle z=1, because the block is denser near the top.

To find it, we compute the first moments: N_x=\iiint_E x\delta\,dV, \qquad N_y=\iiint_E y\delta\,dV, \qquad N_z=\iiint_E z\delta\,dV. For this block,
N_x \amp= \int_0^4\int_0^3\int_0^2 x(2+z)\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \left(\int_0^4 x\,dx\right) \left(\int_0^3 dy\right) \left(\int_0^2(2+z)\,dz\right) \amp= 8\cdot3\cdot6 \amp= 144.
Similarly,
N_y \amp= \left(\int_0^4 dx\right) \left(\int_0^3 y\,dy\right) \left(\int_0^2(2+z)\,dz\right) \amp= 4\cdot\frac92\cdot6 \amp= 108.
For the height moment,
N_z \amp= \int_0^4\int_0^3\int_0^2 z(2+z)\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= 12\int_0^2(2z+z^2)\,dz \amp= 12\left[z^2+\frac{z^3}{3}\right]_0^2 \amp= 12\left(4+\frac83\right) \amp= 80.
Since M=72, the center of mass is \boxed{ (\bar x,\bar y,\bar z) = \left( \frac{N_x}{M},\frac{N_y}{M},\frac{N_z}{M} \right) = \left(2,\frac32,\frac{10}{9}\right). } As expected, \bar z=\frac{10}{9}\gt 1.

figure.caption: When density increases with height, the center of mass moves upward.

statement.p: Let E be a solid with density \delta(x,y,z), and suppose M=\iiint_E \delta\,dV\gt 0. Define the first moments N_x=\iiint_E x\delta\,dV, \qquad N_y=\iiint_E y\delta\,dV, \qquad N_z=\iiint_E z\delta\,dV. Then the center of mass is \boxed{ (\bar x,\bar y,\bar z) = \left( \frac{N_x}{M}, \frac{N_y}{M}, \frac{N_z}{M} \right). }

subsection.p: The condition M\gt 0 matters. A real mass density is nonnegative, and if there is any material at all, then M\gt 0. But for signed quantities, such as electric charge, the same formula can break.

example.title: Why positive total mass is needed

statement.p: Suppose a cube contains equal amounts of positive and negative charge. A signed charge density q might satisfy \iiint_E q\,dV=0. The expression \frac{\iiint_E xq\,dV}{\iiint_E q\,dV} then tries to divide by zero. There may be a meaningful center of positive charge and a meaningful center of negative charge, but there is no center of total signed charge given by this quotient.
So the center of mass formula belongs to a positive finite mass distribution.

subsection.title: Moment of inertia
subsection.p: Center of mass measures balance. Moment of inertia measures resistance to rotation.
A point mass far from an axis is harder to spin than the same point mass near the axis. The distance is squared, so far-away material counts strongly.
If a small volume piece has mass \delta\,dV and its distance from the axis is s, then its small contribution to moment of inertia is s^2\delta\,dV.

definition.title: Moment of inertia about an axis

statement.p: For a solid E with density \delta(x,y,z), the moment of inertia about an axis is \boxed{ I=\iiint_E s^2\delta(x,y,z)\,dV, } where s is the distance from (x,y,z) to the axis.

example.title: A solid cylinder spinning around its axis

statement.p: A solid cylinder has radius 2, height 3, and constant density 5. It spins around its central z-axis.
The mass of the cylinder is M=5\pi(2)^2(3)=60\pi. So I_z=\frac12M(2)^2, which matches the familiar physics formula for a solid cylinder.

figure.caption: For rotation about the z-axis, the distance factor is r^2=x^2+y^2.

subsection.p: Moment of inertia is an integral because a solid is made of many tiny masses, each at its own distance from the axis.

subsection.title: Total charge and total energy
subsection.p: Mass density is nonnegative. Charge density can be positive or negative. Energy density is usually nonnegative.

example.title: Positive and negative charge can cancel

statement.p: A charged cylinder has radius 1, height 2, and charge density q(x,y,z)=z-1. The lower half has negative charge, and the upper half has positive charge.

In cylindrical coordinates, 0\le r\le1, \qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi, \qquad 0\le z\le2. The total charge is
Q \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^1\int_0^2 (z-1)r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta \amp= \left(\int_0^{2\pi}d\theta\right) \left(\int_0^1 r\,dr\right) \left(\int_0^2(z-1)\,dz\right).
But \int_0^2(z-1)\,dz = \left[\frac{z^2}{2}-z\right]_0^2 = 2-2 = 0. Therefore \boxed{ Q=0. } The cylinder is not uncharged everywhere. Its total signed charge is zero because the positive and negative parts cancel.

example.title: Energy stored in a spherical battery material

statement.p: A ball of radius 2 has energy density depending only on distance from the center: u(\rho)=10\left(1-\frac{\rho}{2}\right), \qquad 0\le \rho\le2. The density is largest at the center and drops to 0 at the surface.

subsection.p: The same triple integral notation covers mass, charge, and energy. The units of the integrand tell us what is being added.

subsection.title: Probability in three dimensions
subsection.p: A probability density is another kind of density. Instead of mass per volume or charge per volume, it gives probability per volume.

If a random point is chosen in a solid region E, a probability density p(x,y,z) must satisfy two conditions: p(x,y,z)\ge0 and \iiint_E p(x,y,z)\,dV=1. Then the probability that the point lies in a smaller region A\subseteq E is \boxed{ \operatorname{Prob}(A)=\iiint_A p(x,y,z)\,dV. }

example.title: A random point more likely near the top of a box

statement.p:
Now find the probability that z\le\frac12. This is
\operatorname{Prob}\left(z\le\frac12\right) \amp= \int_0^1\int_0^1\int_0^{1/2} \frac23(1+z)\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \frac23 \left[z+\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_0^{1/2} \amp= \frac23\left(\frac12+\frac18\right) \amp= \frac{5}{12}.
So \boxed{ \operatorname{Prob}\left(z\le\frac12\right)=\frac{5}{12}. } Even though the lower half of the cube has half the volume, it has less than half the probability, because the density increases with z.

subsection.p: The conditions p\ge0, \qquad \iiint_E p\,dV=1 are not cosmetic. Without them, the word probability loses its meaning.

example.title: Why probability densities must be nonnegative and normalized

statement.p: On the unit cube, the function p(x,y,z)=2 is nonnegative, but \iiint_E 2\,dV=2. It assigns total probability 2, not 1, so it is not a probability density.
The function q(x,y,z)=2z-1 has total integral 0, and it is negative when z\lt 1/2. It cannot describe probability, because some regions would receive negative probability.
A probability density is an ordinary density with two extra promises: no negative amounts, and total amount exactly 1.

subsection.p: This last example points beyond three dimensions. A random point might have four coordinates, or ten, or a hundred. A gas molecule has position and velocity. A data point may have many measured features. The adding idea does not stop at three coordinates. What changes is harder to draw: a tiny piece is no longer a rectangle or a box we can picture. It is still a product of tiny coordinate lengths.
